segments.s062-g25: This subordinate clause begins with the relative/conjunction که (that). چون بودتان کار با پور سام is an interrogative/indirect question-like formula meaning “how/if your affair was with the son of Sam.” چون here asks “how/what sort,” بودتان is the past-tense copula ‘you were’ (second-person plural/polite), and کار با پور سام = “the affair/business with the son (پور) Sam (سام).” The whole segment sets up the condition described in the next segment.
segments.s062-g26: بدیدن بهست is an impersonal/nominal phrase meaning ‘it suffices to see’ or ‘seeing is enough’ (بدیدن = to see/meet, بهست = it suffices). ار here is the conditional ‘if,’ and به + آواز و نام marks the manner/instrumental sense ‘by/through reputation (آواز) and name (نام).’ The clause therefore says that, given the preceding condition, merely knowing him by fame and name sufficed.
segments.s062-g27: پری چهره is a fixed poetic compound meaning ‘fairy-faced’ or ‘beautiful-faced’ (پری modifying چهره). هر here functions distributively/emphatically with the group (‘each’ or ‘all’ as in ‘all five’), and بشتافتند is the past plural verb ‘they hastened/rushed.’ Word order places the descriptive phrase before the verb, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s062-g28: چو is temporal ‘when’; با ماه = ‘with the moon’ where ماه is a poetic epithet (‘moonlike one’ = very beautiful person) rather than the calendar month; جای سخن is ‘a place/opportunity for speech/conversation,’ and یافتند is the past plural ‘they found.’ The clause therefore means ‘when they found a place, they spoke with the moonlike one.’
segments.s062-g29: که introduces a descriptive clause ‘that’ (referring back to the person just mentioned). مردیست is an emphatic present/past-state copula ‘he is a man’ (the -است ending fused in poetry). برسان سرو سهی is a poetic comparative: برسان (here ‘reaching up to / as tall as’ in context) + سرو ‘cypress’ (a standard simile for tallness) + سهی ‘dark/black’ (a color epithet). Together they describe his height and hue.
segments.s062-g30: همش (all of him / entirely) modifies the pair زیب و هم فر: زیب = beauty/adornment and فر = royal glory/splendour. The sequence همش زیب و هم فر شاهنشهی says that he is all beauty and that his far (divine/royal radiance) is kingly (شاهنشهی). هم here intensifies/unifies the nouns that follow.
segments.s062-g31: This is a stacked descriptive phrase: همش (= all of him) + رنگ و بوی (‘colour and scent’ — here physical attractiveness/complexion and fragrance) and again همش + قد و شاخ (‘stature and limbs/branches’). The parallelism is typical of Ferdowsi’s cataloguing of a hero’s attributes: first appearance (colour/scent), then form (height/limbs).
segments.s062-g32: سواری is ‘a rider/horseman.’ میان introduces a midpoint ‘between,’ followed by the two poles لاغر (‘lean/slender’) و بر فراخ (فراخ = ‘wide/spacious/ample’). The short clause means his riding/build is intermediate between slender and ample; note بر here is an archaic/poetic particle used in comparative phrasing.
segments.s062-g33: This is a simple simile: the possessor + noun 'دو چشمش' (his two eyes) is followed by the comparative particle 'چو' (like) and the image 'دو نرگس' (two narcissi). The adjective 'قیرگون' ('pitch‑black') comes at the end, describing the eyes; word order (possessor+noun → comparison → adjective) is typical of Ferdowsi's poetic compression.
segments.s062-g34: This line contains two coordinated descriptive clauses compressed without an explicit conjunction: 'لبانش' (his lips) + 'چو' (when/like) + 'بسد' — here 'بسد' is an archaic/formal 3rd‑person present meaning 'he/she/it closes' (i.e. 'when his lips close'), and then a parallel clause 'رخانش چو خون' where 'رخانش' (his face/cheeks/complexion) is compared with 'خون' (blood). The poet omits particles and a verb in the second half for brevity; note that 'بسد' as 'to close' is an archaic use not listed among the provided senses.
segments.s062-g35: 'کف و ساعدش' ('his palm and forearm') is a coordinated noun phrase with the possessive suffix; it is followed by the comparative particle 'چو' and the image 'کف شیر نر' ('the paw of a male lion'). The noun order 'کف شیر نر' is a poetic genitive/attributive construction meaning 'the paw of a male lion' (شیر + نر together specify the lion).
segments.s062-g36: This is a roll of parallel nominal images without an explicit verb (a common epic device): 'هیون ران' (a beast's thigh), 'موبد دل' (a mōbed's heart), 'شاه فر' (a king's glory). Each phrase is elliptical (no copula) and coordinated by 'و' to list further attributes of the hero; the nouns are in the possessive/attributive pattern (modifier before head in this poetic compression).
segments.s062-g37: The adverb 'سراسر' ('throughout/entirely') precedes the predicate 'سپیدست' (an old poetic copular form meaning 'is white'), followed by the possessive 'مویش' ('his hair') and the qualifier 'برنگ' ('in color'). The order places emphasis on the predicate and color: literally, 'entirely white is his hair in color.'
segments.s062-g38: 'از آهو همین است' uses 'از' to mark the source or model ('of/from the gazelle') and asserts with 'است' that this is the case ('this is the same as a gazelle’s'). The following clause 'و این نیست ننگ' is a negation with 'نیست' after demonstrative 'این' — 'and this is not a disgrace' — an explicit statement that such a color/feature is not dishonorable. The two clauses together justify the description.
segments.s062-g39: This noun group 'سر جعد آن پهلوان جهان' places the descriptive 'سر جعد' ('curly head') before the definite epithet 'آن پهلوان جهان' ('that champion of the world'). The order highlights the physical detail (curly head) then names the hero; 'جعد' specifies the hair/locks on the head.
segments.s062-g40: This is a simile introduced by 'چو' ('like'): 'سیمین زره' ('a silvery armor') followed by the locative/prepositional 'بر' ('on/upon') and the image 'گل ارغوان' ('a crimson/purple flower'). Grammatically, the simile compares the hero's curly head to a shining (silvery) armor set upon a deep red/purple blossom — a compressed metaphor typical of epic diction.
segments.s062-g65: عقیق و زبرجد: a simple coordinated noun phrase 'agate and peridot' placed before the verb for poetic ordering. برو ریختند is an archaic/poetic past verb: بر ریختند ‘they poured / scattered (on it)’ with the prepositional prefix بر- realized as برو; the objects precede the verb, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s062-g66: می‌و مشک و عنبر: another coordinated series of nouns 'wine (mi), musk and amber' followed by the past verb برآمیختند 'they mixed/they blended together.' برآمیختند is past indicative plural; the preverb بر- here again gives the sense of joining/bringing together.
segments.s062-g67: همه زر و پیروزه is a quantified noun phrase 'all the gold and turquoise.' The verbless feel is a common poetic ellipsis: the phrase is understood as 'were placed' or 'were set' with جامشان 'their cup' marking the locus. بد here is archaic/poetic and functions as a locative/linking particle ('in' or 'into' their cup) rather than its usual meaning 'bad'—it should be read as a positional particle attaching to جامشان.
segments.s062-g68: به روشن گلاب اندر آشامشان: به + adjective + noun is a prepositional phrase 'with/by bright rosewater'; اندر is another locative 'in/within.' آشامشان is an archaic poetic form of the verb 'to drink' (آشامیدن) used here as a past/actionive clause 'they drank' (the exact morphology is compressed in poetry). The whole phrase says they drank the clear/bright rosewater.
segments.s062-g69: This segment is a list of flowers/colour-words: بنفشه گل 'violet (flower)' where گل is an appositive/clarifying noun; نرگس 'narcissus' and ارغوان 'purple/crimson' continue the enumerative listing. Word order is the typical noun coordination of poetic Persian.
segments.s062-g70: سمن شاخ و سنبل به دیگر کران: another coordinated phrase 'jasmine, branch(es) and hyacinth' followed by the prepositional phrase به دیگر کران 'to/toward the other edge.' شاخ is 'branch' here, and به دیگر کران marks direction/place ('toward the opposite edge').
segments.s062-g71: از آن خانه دخت خورشید روی: a prepositional phrase 'from that house/chamber' (از آن خانه) followed by the noun phrase 'دخت خورشید‌روی' — 'the maiden whose face is like the sun' (خورشید‌روی is a postpositive descriptive compound: 'sun-faced'). The descriptive element is placed after the head noun, as is typical in Persian.
segments.s062-g72: برآمد همی تا به خورشید بوی: برآمد 'emerged/rose' is the simple past; همی is a poetic particle giving immediacy or continuation ('indeed / at once'); تا ... بوی here means 'so that/as far as the sun (the) scent' — idiomatically 'her scent reached even the sun.' تا is used in the sense 'as far as/even to,' and بوی is the abstract noun 'scent' (subject of the reaching).
segments.s062-g57: A noun phrase: پرستنده (the admirer/devotee) is the subject and is followed by a prepositional phrase with با + noun (با بانوی ماه‌روی) meaning 'with / to the moon-faced lady'. In classical Persian the preposition با often marks the addressee; ماه‌روی is a compound adjective (ماه + رو) meaning 'moon-faced/beautiful-faced'.
segments.s062-g58: چنین گفت is a set phrase 'thus said' (literally 'said thus'), where چنین precedes the verb گفت to report speech. کاکنون is an emphatic 'now/at this moment' (کا- intensifier + اکنون). ره چاره جوی is an infinitival/nominal phrase: ره 'way' + چاره 'remedy/solution' + جوی (from جستن) used as a deverbal adjective/noun 'seeker' or verbal noun 'seek' — together meaning 'seek a way/seek a remedy'; word order places the directive after the reporting verb.
segments.s062-g59: This is an optative/wish clause introduced by که. یزدان 'God' is the subject and داد 'gave/grant' is used with a wishing sense. هر آنچت هوا is a fused relative: هر 'whatever/whichever' + آنچت 'that which is yours/what you …' + هوا 'desire', so 'whatever your desire was/ is.' بود is a copula in past form here used classically inside the relative ('that which was your desire'); the clause as a whole expresses a prayerful wish that God grant that desire.
segments.s062-g60: A short benediction: سرانجام 'the outcome/result' + این کار 'this matter' with فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' as predicate adjective, followed by باد, the optative particle meaning 'may it be' (classical wish marker). Word order is Topic (سرانجام این کار) then desiderative predicate (فرخنده باد).
segments.s062-g61: یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite). خانه is the noun 'house' and بودش is the past copula with enclitic pronoun (it/there was). چو 'like/as' introduces a simile, so چو خرم بهار = 'like the joyous spring' where خرم (joyful/lush) modifies بهار (spring). Word order is Subject (a house) — Predicate (was) — Comparative phrase.
segments.s062-g62: This short clause describes the house's appearance: ز (poetic 'from/of') + چهر 'face/appearance' + بزرگان 'the great ones/nobles' — 'from the faces of the great ones' — then برو (imperative/archaic form 'goes/turned' or 'it went') + بر + نگار 'toward the beloved'. In classical Persian ز can mark source or comparison; the sequence implies the house's visage outshone or exceeded the nobles and inclined toward the beloved.
segments.s062-g63: A verbal clause: به + دیبای چینی is a prepositional phrase 'with/ in Chinese brocade' (چینی modifies دیبا); بیاراستند is the past tense plural verb 'they adorned/they decked (it) out'. The verb takes the adornment as its object, and the prepositional به marks the material or dressing used.
segments.s062-g64: طبق‌های زرین is the plural construct 'golden platters/trays' (طبق‌های = 'trays of/plates'), followed by the verb بپیراستند (another past plural 'they adorned/they arranged'), so the two verbs enumerate parallel actions: they dressed (with brocade) and they arrayed golden trays.
segments.s062-g9: که is a subordinating particle linking this clause to what came before (’for/that’). امروز is an adverb of time 'today'. روزی here is a nominal/poetic use (’a day’ / ‘dayhood’) that functions as the topic of the predication. دگر گونه is an adjective phrase 'another / different kind' placed after the head in an elevated poetic order. نیست is the present/past-less copula/negator 'is not / does not exist', negating the whole phrase.
segments.s062-g10: به راه گلان is a prepositional phrase fronted for emphasis: به + راه ('to/on the road/path') with گلان (poetic plural of گل) 'of flowers' qualifying راه. دیو is the noun 'demon/ogre' and واژونه is a postpositive adjective/adverb 'overturned/upside‑down' modifying دیو. نیست is the negated copula 'is not', so the clause says there is no demon in that overturned state on the flowery road.
segments.s062-g11: بهار is the subject 'spring'. آمد is the simple past 'came'. ازگلستان is a fused preposition + noun 'from the rose‑garden' indicating source. گل is the noun 'flower' and چنیم (poetic/older form) follows the noun as a quantity/adverb meaning 'several/many'. The clause lists what came with spring in a compressed, poetic order.
segments.s062-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. روی here means 'surface' (ز روی زمین = 'from the surface of the earth'). زمین is 'earth/ground'. شاخ is 'branch / stalk' and سنبل specifies the plant 'hyacinth', so شاخ سنبل = 'hyacinth stalks/branches'. چنیم again is the quantifier 'many/several'. Word order is poetic: source phrase then the noun phrase with postposed quantifier.
segments.s062-g13: نگهبان is the subject 'the guard'. در here is locative 'in/at' (i.e. 'in the chamber/doorway'); گفت is the past verb 'said' (note: the supplied lexicon senses list shows noun senses, but function here is the verb 'he said'). کامروز is a temporal adverb 'today' and کار is the object/topic 'the affair/work' that the guard is commenting on. The clause sets up the reported speech.
segments.s062-g14: نباید is an impersonal modal/negative 'one must not / should not'. گرفتن is the infinitive 'to take / to seize / to undertake' and together نباید گرفتن expresses prohibition ('must not take up / undertake'). بدان هم شمار is an instruction: بدان is here a compound sense 'to that / in that regard' (spelled as ب + دان in the segmentation) and دان is used as 'consider/know'; هم is 'also' and شمار is the imperative/nominal sense 'count' or 'tally'. Overall: 'do not undertake it; count him/them as well.'
segments.s062-g15: که is the subordinating particle introducing the subordinated report or explanation. زال is the personal name 'Zāl' and سپهبد is an appositive/title 'spahbed' (general). بکابل uses the prefix بـ + place name to indicate location 'in/to Kabul'. نبود is the past negative of بودن 'was not'. The clause therefore states that Zāl the spahbed was not (present) in Kabul.
segments.s062-g16: سراپرده is 'inner chamber / inner tent' (the private part of a tent/palace). شاه is 'the king' and زابل qualifies/locates him (the king of Zabul); the construct is possessive/attributive: 'the inner chamber of the king of Zabul'. نبود is again the past negative 'was not'. The clause says that that inner chamber was empty / the king was not there.
segments.s062-g1: رسیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past (they arrived); Persian poetry often places the verb before its subject, so خوبان (the subject) follows the verb. به درگاه کاخ is a prepositional phrase ('to/at the palace threshold') marking the destination/place.
segments.s062-g2: به دست اندرون is an idiomatic locative/instrumental phrase meaning 'in (their) hand(s)' or 'held in hand'; the clause omits an explicit verb (elliptical) so هر یک از گل دو شاخ ('each of the two‑pronged flowers') is understood as the object each person holds. Note poetic word order and the attributive 'دو شاخ' modifying گل.
segments.s062-g3: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction from نگه‌داشتن/نگاه کردن meaning 'kept watch'; the verb (کرد) appears before its noun subject دربان (the gatekeeper) — a common poetic inversion. برآراست جنگ is an adjectival/predicative phrase ('the battle was arrayed/adorned'); برآراست is an archaic/poetic perfective form meaning 'was prepared/arrayed' modifying جنگ.
segments.s062-g4: This is two short predications joined by و. زبان کرد گستاخ uses کرد as a light verb/coprula: 'the tongue (speech) acted/spoke impudently' (زبان as subject, گستاخ a predicative adjective). دل کرد تنگ is a parallel idiom: 'the heart became constricted/sorrowful' (دل + کرد + تنگ). The repetition of کرد is typical of classical Persian to form stative/resultative senses.
segments.s062-g5: که introduces a subordinate (conditional/indirect) clause here ('that/if'). بی‌گه is an adverb meaning 'untimely' or 'at an inopportune hour'. ز درگاه بیرون شوید literally 'you go out from the threshold' — شوید is the present/subjunctive 2nd/3rd‑person plural of شدن used to express the (potential/future) act 'you go/leave'.
segments.s062-g6: شگفت آیدم is an impersonal idiom 'it will be a wonder/it will astonish me' (آیدم literally 'it comes to me'). تا introduces the subordinate clause giving what astonishes the speaker; شما چون شوید 'how/what you will become' uses چون = 'how' and شود (subjunctive) for the prospective/uncertain result. Overall: 'It will astonish me to see how you fare if you leave untimely.'
segments.s062-g7: بتان here is used for the beautiful women (figurative 'idols/beautiful ones') as the subject. پاسخش is 'their/its answer' (پاسخ + possessive -ش), and را is the definite object marker; بیاراستند is the poetic past plural 'they arranged/prepared' (بیاراستن = to arrange, prepare).
segments.s062-g8: به تنگی دل is an idiomatic causal/attitudinal phrase: 'with hearts tightened' i.e., 'with sorrow/reluctance' (به marks the circumstance/cause and تنگی دل is figurative). از جای برخاستند is a simple past plural clause 'they rose from their place(s)' (برخاستند = they rose up, departed).
segments.s062-g17: نبینید is a negated perfect/present-stative form from دید/بین; in this narrative it functions as a negative statement ('they did not see' / 'it was not seen'). کز is the classical fused conjunction meaning 'that/from which' introducing the clause; کاخ کابل is a genitive phrase 'the palace of Kabul.' خدای here denotes the exalted person (literally 'God' or 'lord') mentioned as coming from that palace.
segments.s062-g18: به ... اندر آرد is a verbal phrase: به + noun + اندر + آورد(آرد) = 'bring/put into'. به here = 'into/toward', اندر = 'in/within', آرد = 3rd-person singular present/habitual 'he brings' (classical narrative). بشبگیر is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'for the night' / 'to spend the night', and پای ('foot') is the direct object: 'bring the foot into the saddle to stay the night.'
segments.s062-g19: اگرتان is the conditional 'if' (اگر) with the enclitic -تان addressing 'you/your'; ببیند is a subjunctive/present form of 'to see' used here in a protasis ('if ... is seen'). چنین گل بدست is a noun phrase 'such a flower in (the) hand' (بدست = 'in the hand').
segments.s062-g20: کند is the 3rd-person singular present/habitual of کردن 'to do/make' used as the main verb in the apodosis ('he will make/do'). بر زمین‌تان = 'upon your ground/land' (بر + زمین + possessive -تان). هم آنگاه is a temporal/conclusive sequence 'even then / in that case / then', and پست is an adjective/adverb meaning 'low, humbled' describing the resulting state.
segments.s062-g21: شدند is the simple past/pluperfect 3rd-person plural 'they became/went/entered' (classical narrative past). اندر ایوان = 'into the iwan/portico.' بتان طراز is a descriptive/genitive phrase: بتان (literary 'the beauties' or 'idols' used as metaphor for beautiful women) and طراز modifies them as 'ornately fashioned/arrayed' (i.e., 'the ornate iwan of the beauties').
segments.s062-g22: نشستند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they sat' (classical narrative). و simply links clauses ('and'); با ماه is a prepositional phrase 'with the moon' (here idiomatic: addressing or confiding to the moon), and گفتند راز = 'they spoke/said a secret' (گفتند is the verb 'they spoke', راز is the object 'secret').
segments.s062-g23: نهادند is the past 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'they placed/laid (down)'. دینار و گوهر are the direct objects 'dinar(s) and gems/jewels.' به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of' (they placed the valuables before her).
segments.s062-g24: بپرسید (here a past/interrogative form) = 'they asked'; رودابه is the addressee's name. از introduces the content/topic of the question ('about/regarding'), and کم و بیش is an idiom 'little and more' = 'less or more / how much' (i.e., whether she wants less or more).
segments.s062-g41: که introduces a clause ('that/for/as'), followed by گویی which functions here as an evidential/optative 'it seems / as if' (poetic use). همی is an older continuous/emphatic particle placed before the verb; خود is an emphatic 'yourself'; چنان is an adverb 'thus/so'; بایدی is an archaic second-person singular form of باید meaning 'you must/ought to be'. The whole segment reads literally 'that it seems indeed you yourself must be so.'
segments.s062-g42: وگر is the conditional 'if / but if'; نیستی is the archaic second-person negative copula 'you are not'; مهر is the object 'love/affection'; نفزایدی is an archaic negative subjunctive/jussive (third‑person impersonal result) meaning 'would not increase / will not grow'. Together this gives the consequence: 'but if you are not, love will not increase.'
segments.s062-g43: به دیار تو is a prepositional phrase 'to your land/homeland' placed before the verb for poetic word order. داده‌ایمش is a perfective form with an enclitic object (–یمش) meaning 'I/we have given it (to him/it/them)'; نوید is the direct object 'news/Good tidings'. Literal order: 'To your land I have given the news.'
segments.s062-g44: ز ما ('from us') introduces the source; بازگشتست is a nominalized/existential construction 'there is a return / a return has occurred' (poetic inversion); دل پرامید is a verbless clause 'the heart hopeful' (i.e. 'the heart is full of hope'). Together: 'From us there is a return, (so) the heart is hopeful.'
segments.s062-g45: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. چاره کار مهمان is a noun phrase 'a remedy/plan for the guest' (چاره modifies کار, with مهمان specifying whose affair). بساز is the second‑person singular imperative of ساختن 'make/prepare/arrange'. The verb at the end is the imperative addressing the addressee to arrange the guest's matter.
segments.s062-g46: بفرمای here is a polite imperative or hortative 'please say/command' (or reported 'he commanded'); تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; بر چه is an interrogative prepositional phrase 'on what / for what reason'; گردیم is a first‑person‑plural present/jussive form of گشتن/گردیدن used here as 'we may turn/return'; باز is the adverb 'again'. The clause asks to be told the grounds on which they should return.
segments.s062-g47: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said'. با بندگان is 'to/with the servants' (با + noun). سرو بن appears as a compound/name or epithet attached to the addressees (poetic name/title) — it functions as the identifier of those servants. Word order places the manner/adverb and speech verb before the addressee phrase in a typical narrative inversion.
segments.s062-g48: که introduces the reported content ('that'); دیگر شدستی is an archaic verbal phrase: دیگر 'other/different' plus شدستی, an older second‑person past/perfective form meaning 'you have become / you are changed' (the -ستی ending is an older 2nd‑person marker). به رای و سخن is a prepositional phrase 'in (your) counsel/opinion and speech' — رای = counsel/opinion and سخن = speech/utterance. The clause therefore reports 'that you are now changed in counsel and speech.'
segments.s062-g49: همان زال is a noun phrase 'that/same Zāl' (همان used as a determiner). کو here functions as an archaic/poetic relative ('who/whom'), followed by the adjectival past participle مرغ پرورده 'bird‑reared' (مرغ + پرورده) describing Zāl. بود is the past copula 'was' closing the relative clause.
segments.s062-g50: چنان is an adverb of degree 'so/such', modifying the following predicate. پیر سر is a descriptive phrase 'old/gray‑headed' (پیر = old, سر = head). The clause has two coordinated predicates separated by و: 'پیر سر بود' ('was old/gray‑headed') and 'پژمرده بود' ('was withered'), each with the past copula بود for emphasis.
segments.s062-g51: به دیدار شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came into view/appeared' (به + noun دیدار + شد 'became/happened'); the preposition here indicates movement/appearance into sight. چون introduces a simile 'like', and گل ارغوان is the image 'a crimson/purple flower' modifying how he appeared.
segments.s062-g52: This is a string of nominal/epithet phrases describing the person: سهی قد ('dark/tawny of stature'), followed by زیبا رخ ('beautiful‑faced') and then پهلوان ('a champion/hero'). The coordinating و links the descriptors; there is no copula because these are attributive epithets.
segments.s062-g53: رخ من is a possessive phrase 'my face' (word order: noun then possessor). به پیشش 'in his presence/before him' is a prepositional phrase specifying where. بیاراستی is an archaic/past form meaning 'you adorned/arranged' (second‑person past), so the whole segment says 'you set my face aright before him.'
segments.s062-g54: به گفتار 'by/with speech' (using words) marks the means; و links to a subsequent clause. زان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereafter', and بهاخواستی is a past verb meaning 'you asked a price/demanded payment' (بها = price, خواستی = you desired/asked).
segments.s062-g55: همی is a durative/adverbial particle indicating continuous or habitual action ('kept on/was continually'). گفت here functions as 'he said/spoke' (an older poetic form). و connects to the descriptive clause لب را پر از خنده داشت: را marks لب as definite object/topic, پر از + noun = 'full of …', and داشت is an aspectual/past auxiliary meaning 'had/was having'—so 'his lips were full of a smile/laughter.'
segments.s062-g56: رخان (plural/collective of رخ here) is the subject 'the cheeks/faces'; هم‌چو گلنار is a simile 'like a pomegranate‑blossom'; آگنده is an adjectival past participle 'filled/full', and داشت again functions as a past auxiliary 'had'—together: 'his cheeks, like a pomegranate blossom, were full.'

sentences.s062-s13: That, if your affair with the son of Sam was like that, it was enough to know him by his reputation and name.
sentences.s062-s14: All five, fairy-faced, rushed forward; when they found a place they spoke with the moonlike one.
sentences.s062-s15: That he is a man reaching up to a cypress in stature, dark (in hue) — wholly beautiful and wholly endowed with kingly splendour.
sentences.s062-s16: All of him in colour and scent, and all of him in stature and limbs — a horseman whose build is between slender and ample.
sentences.s062-s17: His two eyes, like two narcissi, were pitch‑black; when his lips closed, his cheeks were like blood.
sentences.s062-s18: His palm and forearm were like the paw of a male lion; his thigh like a beast's, his heart like a mōbed's, and his royal glory like a king's.
sentences.s062-s19: His hair was entirely white in color; it is like that of a gazelle — that is so, and it is no shame.
sentences.s062-s20: The curly head of that champion of the world is like a silvery armor upon a crimson flower.
sentences.s062-s33: They scattered agate and peridot upon it; they mixed wine with musk and amber.
sentences.s062-s34: All the gold and turquoise (were) in their cups; they drank bright rosewater within (them).
sentences.s062-s35: Violet and rose, narcissus and crimson; jasmine, branches and hyacinth toward the other edge.
sentences.s062-s36: From that chamber the sun-faced maiden emerged, so that even the sun caught her scent.
sentences.s062-s29: The admirer said to the moon-faced lady: "Now seek a way and a remedy."
sentences.s062-s30: May God grant whatever your desire is; may the outcome of this affair be auspicious.
sentences.s062-s31: There was a certain house like the joyful spring; its visage outshone the great ones and turned toward the beloved.
sentences.s062-s32: They adorned it with Chinese brocade and set out golden platters.
sentences.s062-s5: For today there is no other kind of day; on the flowery road no demon is overturned.
sentences.s062-s6: Spring came from the rose‑garden — many flowers; from the surface of the earth many hyacinth branches (sprouted).
sentences.s062-s7: The guard inside said, 'Today this affair must not be undertaken; count him among them as well.'
sentences.s062-s8: That Zāl the spahbed was not in Kabul; the king of Zabul was not in the inner tent.
sentences.s062-s1: The beauties reached the palace threshold; each held in her hand a two‑pronged flower.
sentences.s062-s2: The gatekeeper kept watch and the battle was arrayed; the tongue spoke boldly, and the heart grew constricted.
sentences.s062-s3: That if you should go out of the palace untimely, it would astonish me to see what will become of you.
sentences.s062-s4: The beauties prepared their reply; with heavy hearts they rose from their places.
sentences.s062-s9: They did not see that from Kabul’s palace a lord would put his foot into the saddle to spend the night.
sentences.s062-s10: If such a flower is seen in your hand, then he will abase (you) on your own ground.
sentences.s062-s11: They went into the ornate portico of the beauties, sat down, and confided their secret to the moon.
sentences.s062-s12: They set dinars and jewels before her, and asked Rudabeh whether (she wanted) little or much.
sentences.s062-s21: It is as if you yourself must be so; but if you are not, love will not increase.
sentences.s062-s22: I have given your land the news: our return is at hand, and the heart is hopeful.
sentences.s062-s23: Now prepare a solution for the guest; tell us so that we may know on what grounds we should return.
sentences.s062-s24: Thus he spoke to the servants of Sarvban, saying, 'You have now become different in counsel and speech.'
sentences.s062-s25: That same Zāl, who had been reared by a bird, was so gray‑headed and withered.
sentences.s062-s26: He appeared before (them) like a crimson flower, dark of stature, fair‑faced and a hero.
sentences.s062-s27: You had made my face presentable before him with words, and afterward you demanded a price for it.
sentences.s062-s28: He kept speaking, and his lips were full of a smile; his cheeks, like a pomegranate‑blossom, were filled (with color).
